name: Asking Questions
summary: Three ways to ask a question in French, from casual to formal, plus the question words.
explanation:
French offers three levels of question. The most casual is just rising intonation: Tu viens ? (You're coming?). The everyday standard adds est-ce que to the front: Est-ce que tu viens ? The most formal inverts the verb and subject: Viens-tu ?

For open questions, place a question word at the start: qui (who), que / qu'est-ce que (what), où (where), quand (when), pourquoi (why), comment (how), combien (how much).

Beginners can rely on intonation and est-ce que for almost everything, saving inversion for writing and formal speech.

examples.en: Do you have the time? (est-ce que)

commonMistakes: Adding 'do' as in English: it is Tu parles ?, not Est-ce que tu fais parler ?
Forgetting the hyphen in inversion: it is parles-tu ?, not parles tu ?
Mixing est-ce que with inversion in the same question. Use one method.
Using que alone at the start of a spoken question. Prefer qu'est-ce que.
Dropping the question word. Open questions need qui, où, quand, and so on.
tip: Start with est-ce que. Putting it in front of any statement turns it into a clear, polite question.
